Note: Totals may not sum due to rounding Nigeria: Facing the Issues, Building Better Quality Growth While the Group delivered strong overall results, we also took decisive action in areas where performance needed to improve, most notably in Nigeria. Nigeria remains one of Ecobank's largest market opportunities, and transforming the business there remains an important part of our GTR strategy. At the same time, it remains a business that needs active, focused management attention. In 2025, it was one of our biggest priorities. Ecobank Nigeria has met the Central Bank of Nigeria's minimum capital requirement of N200 billion for national banks.

This requirement was met in December 2024. As communicated previously, Ecobank Nigeria's capital adequacy ratio is below the regulatory minimum of 10%. This is a matter that the Board and Management of Ecobank Nigeria are actively addressing through a combination of measures, while providing regular updates to the Central Bank of Nigeria. In the fourth quarter of 2025, as part of our measures to exit the Central Bank of Nigeria's regulatory forbearance, we took significant impairments in Nigeria, particularly within the CIB portfolio. This resulted in a pre-tax loss of $31 million for the year and a non-performing loan ratio of 42.1%. These actions made the turnaround more demanding in the near term, but they were necessary. They brought greater transparency to legacy issues and allowed us to address the difficult matters directly rather than carry them forward. What matters now is execution. We are working closely with the regulator.

We are actively managing capital and asset-quality issues and we are reshaping the business towards areas where we see better-quality growth. That shift is already visible. In 2025, CCB in Nigeria grew by 23% year on year, driven by deeper client engagement and growing transaction volumes. We still have work to do, but the path is clear: deal with the legacy issues, restore compliance, and build a better-quality business underneath. Growth, Transformation and Returns in Action Our Growth, Transformation and Returns strategy remains the right framework for the next phase of Ecobank's development. Growth means deepening customer relationships in the businesses and markets where we can win sustainably. Transformation means having the courage to reinvent the way we work and the solutions we provide our clients, while leveraging evolving technology and artificial intelligence solutions to serve our clients better. In doing so, we have sharpened our clarity of focus in each market so that we can compete more effectively.

We have also upgraded our risk frameworks and appetite in line with changing 1. Who We Are 2. Group Chairman and Management 3. Corporate Governance 4. Risk Management Ecobank Group Annual Report 2025 geopolitical and macroeconomic fundamentals. These actions enhance the way we run the Group. Our Returns pillar focuses on having a disciplined approach to capital management and allocation, converting our scale, network and capabilities into stronger and more sustainable shareholder value. In particular, we have focused on and made significant progress on dividend upstreaming by subsidiaries, which has grown from $217 million in 2024 to $303 million in 2025. This has strengthened our central liquidity and capital position, supporting our proposed dividend payment. In 2025, we made progress across all three areas. We improved our payments, cash management and trade finance capabilities. We continued to invest in technology and digital platforms to support faster product delivery, better integration across markets and a stronger customer experience.

We also took steps to improve how the Group works internally, simplifying processes, strengthening governance and making execution more disciplined. Across the Group, we remained focused on doing the simple things well, with consistency, accountability and better followthrough. We are not getting distracted; we are taking the right difficult decisions and executing with agility and discipline. Purpose Beyond Banking Our purpose also extends beyond banking. Through the Ecobank Foundation, we continued to support social impact across Africa, with programmes focused on health, education and gender equality. In 2025, the Foundation strengthened its role as a platform for partnerships, including work on health and nutrition, inclusive digital education, and women's financial empowerment. This reflects a simple belief: stronger communities help build stronger economies. In education, the Foundation concluded its three-year "Transforming Africa Through Education" campaign, exceeding its initial targets by establishing 66 IT laboratories and training 46,414 children and young people across the continent.

Through the enhanced Ellevate programme, launched in nine African countries in March 2025, Ecobank also continued to support women entrepreneurs with financing, mentoring and advisory services. These initiatives are part of how the Group connects financial inclusion with broader economic inclusion. Focused on the Next Phase As we look ahead to 2026, our focus remains on disciplined execution. Subject to market conditions, we expect net revenue growth in the low-to-mid single digits, with operating expense growth in the low single digits. We will continue to manage the business towards a cost-to-income ratio of 48% to 50%, while reducing cost of risk and improving asset quality over time. How we deliver that is just as important as the targets themselves. In CIB, we see further room to deepen wallet share, grow transaction banking, expand trade finance and build sector expertise in the industries that matter most across our markets. In CCB, we see opportunities to grow customer numbers, deepen primary banking relationships, increase transactional activity and scale lending carefully within a stronger risk framework.

In payments, we continue to see significant opportunity across domestic payments, cross-border flows, remittances and Banking-as-a-Service, supported by investment in technology, partnerships and our central platform. These targets are linked to clear delivery levers across the business: stronger customer acquisition, deeper client relationships, better transaction flows, continued digital investment, tighter cost discipline and more active risk management. That is how we intend to strengthen returns, improve earnings quality and continue building a bank that is more efficient, more connected and better positioned for the future. Building the Ecobank of the Future As we mark 40 years of Ecobank, I do not see an institution that has reached its destination. I see one becoming more focused on what it was built to do. Africa still needs connection, but now at a new level: faster, more digital, more secure, more integrated and more inclusive. That is the work in front of us.

To our employees, thank you for what you deliver every day. To our regulators, thank you for your partnership and your challenge to keep improving. To our customers, thank you for trusting us in the moments that matter. And to our shareholders, thank you for your trust, patience, scrutiny and belief. Our task now is clear, even if demanding to deliver: to execute consistently across 34 African markets so that customers choose Ecobank first, every time. Since the launch of the Growth, Transformation and Returns strategy in November 2023, the Company has continued to drive sustainable business and financial performance for the long-term value for all stakeholders, primarily across its four geographical regions, namely, Anglophone West Africa (AWA), Central, Eastern and Southern Africa (CESA), Francophone West Africa (UEMOA) and Nigeria, and its two business segments, Corporate and Investment Banking and Consumer and Commercial Banking, leveraging on transformation initiatives. A comprehensive review of the Group's businesses in 2025 can be found in the Annual Report's Business and Financial Review section on pages 116 to 135. Highlight of results ETI, as a holding company reported a profit after tax of $114 million for the financial year that ended on 31 December 2025. Detailed results for this period can be found in the Parent Company's financial statements on pages 266 to 269 of this Annual Report. The Board of Directors approved the Parent Company's financial statements and the Group's consolidated financial statements for the year ended 31 December 2025 at a meeting held on 12 March 2026. Messrs. Papa Madiaw Ndiaye, Jeremy Awori and Ayo Adepoju were authorised to sign the accounts on behalf of the Board. Both ETI's and the Ecobank Group's accounts are prepared in accordance with International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS). Share capital The Authorised Capital of ETI is $1,250,000,000 as of 31 December 2025. Trading in ETI shares: Approximately 845,948,601 units of ETI ordinary shares were traded on the NGX, Lagos, the BRVM, Abidjan, and the GSE, Accra, during 2025. Major changes in shareholding In accordance with its strategic decision to focus on their core markets in Southern and Eastern Africa, Nedbank Group divested its shareholding from the Company.
